title: A Report on Lebanon
speaker: Teenie Lyro
source_files: A Report on Lebanon - Teenie Lyro.txt

outline.title: The Call to Sacrifice and Serve
subpoints: Calling on young people and others facing big decisions to lay their sacrifice — youth, singleness, comfort — at the foot of the cross
The feelings of fear and resignation before service are normal and part of the journey, not disqualifying
Everything surrendered to Christ is worth it in the eternal perspective, even if you cannot see the fruit immediately
Early years: high-volume ministry with hundreds of people, learning to say goodbye, discovering that God would be enough

outline.title: Ministry to the Forgotten: Children with Disabilities
subpoints: Work began with one resistant child who eventually became an affectionate companion — the hardest battles are often the ones you end up loving most
Goal was to see children with disabilities recognized by their families and community and enrolled in schools
These children became best friends with one another, creating unexpected community across families
One week before leaving, a child demonstrated that she could read — a moment of extraordinary grace and joy

outline.title: Life in a Pressure Cooker: The Ordinary and the Extraordinary
subpoints: Much of missionary life is ordinary: morning coffee, normal meals, driving children to appointments, Tuesday-Thursday breakfast with a friend
The economic collapse of Lebanon made even simple meals a splurge; one family ate meat only on Thursdays because of the missionary's visits
The country underwent a revolution; COVID closed borders in 2020; gas lines stretched for twelve hours in hundred-degree heat
Low-volume relational ministry created space for women to be honest about their lives and opened doors for the gospel

outline.title: Loneliness, Identity, and God's Faithfulness in Exile
subpoints: Missionaries often feel they do not fully belong anywhere — neither in the country of service nor back home
God showed his faithfulness through a Zoom prayer meeting with an unknown woman in the same situation — the assurance of not being alone
Psalm 127 and the experience of the church split taught that unless the Lord builds the house, those who labor build in vain
Jeremiah 29:11 was written to people in exile — we are all exiles in this world (1 Peter 1) and God's plans to prosper his people stand

summary:
This is a firsthand missionary report to the congregation from Teenie Lyro, who served approximately four years in Lebanon. The presentation takes the form of a slide-show talk with photos narrating her ministry journey. She begins with an appeal to young people facing decisions about service, encouraging them that the fear and sense of sacrifice they feel is normal — everything laid at the foot of the cross for Christ's sake is worth it in the eternal perspective.

The bulk of the report describes two phases of ministry. The first was high-volume work serving hundreds of people, learning the language, building relationships, and learning to say goodbye as people moved in and out of her life. The second phase shifted to low-volume, intensive relational ministry focused on a small group of families and a cluster of children with disabilities. The project of working with disabled children — getting them recognized, enrolled in schools, and building community with one another — was the most fruitful and emotional part of the ministry. The week before leaving, one of the children demonstrated that she could read, prompting tears of shock and joy.

The report is honest about the hardships: Lebanon's economic collapse, a national revolution, the COVID border shutdown of 2020, gas lines stretching twelve hours in hundred-degree heat. One family ate meat only on the Thursday the missionary visited. The economic crisis that reduced normal meals to a luxury also opened doors, as desperate people began to let down their facades and speak honestly — which opened conversations about the gospel.

Teenie reflects on the spiritual challenges of missionary life: the experience of not belonging anywhere, the loneliness of living alone in a foreign country, the grief of the church split that felt like years of labor had crumbled. She found encouragement through a Psalm 127 reflection — unless the Lord builds the house — and through an unexpected Zoom prayer meeting with a stranger in an identical situation. She closes by drawing on Jeremiah 29:11 in its proper context: a promise to exiles, and since all believers are exiles (1 Peter 1), the promise of God's plans to prosper his people applies to this ministry and to her ongoing transition.
transcript:
Good. Okay. Well, I want to go back to this girl. And I want to talk to you young people — or you old people. You guys were there when I was making this decision. It was not an easy decision. I think it is really easy to think of all the things you are going to give up and the ways it is going to be hard, the places where you might have to sacrifice. And I am not just talking about going to a different country. Maybe you have a decision in your life where service is going to always need to call you to give something up. And I am going to tell you, people that are maybe even feeling ashamed that they have some sort of reservation, that there is something scary about that, that you feel like you have to sacrifice — that is okay. That is part of the journey. But everything that you are willing to sacrifice at the foot of the cross, whether it is your youth, your singleness, your comfortableness, whatever it may be, it is worth it in the eternal perspective. You might not see it here on Earth. I am not going to say it is going to turn out perfectly. But for the sake of the cross, it is worth it. So if that is something that you are thinking about, if you are wondering if you should take that step, I am encouraging you to do that.

This girl was young, naive, had no idea what was coming for her. But that is a really good thing. I think I would have just gotten back on a plane, I do not know.

And honestly, it is really hard for me to go back, even when I was trying to prepare this and talk about the early years. I think when the church split happened, it felt like a lot of that work just crumbled. And Psalm 127 kept coming up in my mind: unless the Lord builds the house, he who labors labors in vain. And that was a lot of years of labor, a lot of years. And I think these years were definitely high-volume, high-volume ministry. It was every day, all day, non-stop, hundreds of people. And I learned a lot through this. I learned that I was going to survive in this transition. I learned how to deal with all sorts of people. I could not speak the language at that time — there was no turning it off. I learned how to live in a different country. I started building a community. And I learned how to say goodbye. One of the hard lessons learned early on was saying goodbye.

There was this young boy. In my first maybe year there, he and his brothers practically lived with me. I took care of them. I made them dinner. I did their homework with them. I did everything for them. And then one day they were gone. Just learning to say goodbye — to say the Lord is going to bring new people, the seeds have been planted — that was definitely really hard.

But like I said, in these early stages I dug roots. God showed me that he was going to be enough. When I thought the challenges were insurmountable, I did not know that they would get harder. But it is one thing at a time. And I think these were my Mary and Martha years. I kept going back to that story over and over again. Martha was the one that was working in the house. And Jesus called her out and said, because your sister was focused on him, you are missing it. I was definitely Martha. And you kind of do not want to hear that you need to stop working and focus. But I think God was really working in me in those years. And a big thing is that all these pictures from my early years — these were all super important relationships that developed. God put people in my life that would be there through the whole process, that would come back over and over and over again. Some of these people came to Christ.

And I remember there was a day where I was just so discouraged — all of this, I am here, what happened? And the week before, one of these girls in the picture got baptized. And a man came, and he did not really know me, and he said, if nothing else, if you walk away and nothing else is there to show, but this one girl got baptized and you were able to be part of it — was it worth it for the one person, giving all of that up? And you have to keep coming back to say the answer is yes. And I am sorry, I know this is going to be really emotional the whole time. This is a little bit of processing for me as well. But you guys have been around, you know.

So God has been faithful in all of that, above and beyond.

## Life in Lebanon: The Ordinary and the Extraordinary

And there has been a lot of hard things since the beginning. This is a video of one of the days that I stood in the gas line — this is hundreds of cars, twelve hours. It was a hundred degrees out, twelve hours. I waited to get any gas. And I think that we can hyper-focus on all these really hard moments. And I talk about the hard moments because they exist. These crazy stories — I just start feeling like I cannot do them justice, I cannot explain them well enough. They are the things that come up when you talk.

But also so much of my life was just ordinary. And yeah, it was ordinary in a pressure cooker. But you know, I still woke up every day. And there was coffee every morning when we had it. But it was filled with a lot of normal Monday moments, which means it was filled with a lot of normal Monday growth grades. Each morning there was mercy. There was a chance to try again. There was forgiveness. There were all these things that we take for granted. During that time I lived in four different apartments. Maybe not that normal for four years. But it went in strides. I owned a dog. I ate a lot of ice cream. And I also made a lot of normal, boring things — like oatmeal and pancakes. I am trying to stress that it was just a lot of time where you are just waking up and doing your job and doing what you have each day, mixed in with all of the other stuff.

And I spent a lot of quiet evenings with friends. This was really important to me. From here on, you are going to see a switch to the last year or so where my work went from really high volume to really just focused. All the same people in all the pictures. We spent a lot of quiet evenings together, a lot of just talking, talking about everything. Eating good food and eating leftovers. And I have come to the realization that everybody needs leftovers people in their lives — people that you can go to their house and they will just serve you what they have in their fridge, and just talk to you about their day and be able to share in the trials and the victories of life. This is how we got to be together. So this was really important. And if the family eats off the floor with you, that means they are comfortable with you.

We also spent a lot of time in the car. You guys were so generous and helped me raise funds to get this car. And she had her moments but she got us through a lot. I spent a lot of time in the car. All these kids, they do not live close to each other — it is about an hour or an hour and work your way in. So there were a lot of good conversations, a lot of exhausting conversations. Zaza, who shows up in a lot of the pictures, can have the same conversation for an hour straight, the same question: are you coming to pick me up on Wednesday? Are you coming to pick me up on Wednesday? Are you coming to pick me up on Wednesday? So a lot of that in the car. But just a lot of time of prayer, driving with the windows down, growing with these kids in the car.

We did a lot of stuff like school pickups. Doctors appointments. Just going for a ride, eating chips. We would have our Tuesday, Thursday breakfast every week with my friend, and her shop — she owned a shop downtown. So just becoming part of the local community. This is not the type of breakfast we normally eat. Normally you see bread and herbs and olives and tea. This was a special occasion. That is my weakness in friends, so it shows up.

And there was a lot of beauty in these moments. These are the moments that made it feel like this became my life. These kids became the kids that I loved. And these quiet background moments — and a lot of food. Once again, this is not the food we always ate. But this is a lot of the traditional food: stuffed grape leaves, eggplants, zucchini and whatever it is. A lot happens in the Arab culture around a table.

And this got really hard once the economic crisis hit. The country collapsed. We had an entire revolution. People could not afford to put food on their table. So this went from really normal to a splurge. These kind of meals probably cost ten dollars per table. But this was a huge splurge. So that is not how we normally ate. I had one family that I went to every Thursday. They could only eat meat one week in forty. And after going for about a year, they told me: we only eat meat on Thursday because you are coming. So this was not how it always is, but you learned to economize. Things get harder, one little step at a time.

And then I just — like I said — it switched to low-volume relational ministry, which meant a lot of conversation. And people began to see me as one of them because I was not living in a bubble necessarily. I still did not have power when everybody else did not have power. I was still sitting on the floor in basic stuff. So this opened the door for people to be honest. And this opened the door for a lot of need — because hurting people, once they feel like they have someone to talk to, oh, do they like to talk. And so this was both fulfilling and obviously exhausting in a way. It was going going going. But just to know that I could walk into somebody's house, these women, and they would start talking about how they really feel, they would not feel like they had to have a facade — this opened a lot of doors for the gospel. This opened a lot of just good conversation.

But the gospel is polarizing. The gospel is not going to make people comfortable. So this open conversation did get me banned from some people's houses. It had its good moments and its hard moments.

I have one friend whose kids these are in the picture. She has four kids. The oldest one is twelve. He provides for the family because his father does not really work. So the twelve-year-old provides for the family. And she was just in the depths of depression when I first met her. I think she still struggles every day. But just being in her house and watching her fight for joy — making the decision that she would fight for joy for the sake of her children — has been a real testimony. Because she is in one of those situations where it is bad. And all you can say is, this is really bad. It is just not one of those situations where you can change her life in a day. She has to make the decision that she is going to live for joy in this situation. So that has been a huge testimony through all of this.

## Ministry to Children with Disabilities

And then obviously the biggest thing in my life has been the kids. It is hard to explain the depth of how much I have come to love each one of them. You just see all of them and they are a handful. That one up there — she put transmission fluid in the back of my car. She calls me twenty-seven times a day still. That one is the one who enacts justice on all the other kids, which means I have had to send him home for giving other kids black eyes. The one in the back is just so slow — you have to wait ten minutes for him to walk the same distance as everybody else. She bites a lot and he screams a lot.

But then you can also look at it and see that the one who is slow is the sweetest kindest boy that you love. And he remembers everything. He is so smart. And you see how he shows you that he loves you in his own quiet ways. And Zaza — you will never find anyone who wants to be your best friend more. And Mela, she bites, but she is also really, really sweet.

So we have met a lot of challenges and a lot of victories together. Some days it feels like a lot more challenges. Getting called in because someone got called up or acted up in school or whatever it may be. But this was the forgotten demographic. It was a fight to convince even their parents, because their parents obviously loved them so much they did not want to put them on anybody's hands. So it took months to do this. And then through that, to convince them to put these kids into schools and whatever thing — this was a fight to have these kids, who very few people saw, be seen.

And what ended up being created was something that I just could not have imagined. These kids became best friends with each other. I would take them to each other's houses and they would just be so proud and they would talk about each other to their other friends and people. And within the families too, their parents and siblings — this community was created. And I just do not know... you try to do something and I had a lot of failed plans. I think for all intents and purposes, with the church split and everything that happened, it did not turn out how I wanted it to. But God was so gracious in it. And he filled the gaps beyond. And I know that he will continue to do so. And he will continue working through these families.

And there was a moment, like a week before I left, when she showed me that she knew how to read. And I literally started sobbing, because I did not think these kids would end up back in school, let alone getting enough attention. But they can read. [Video plays.] Oh, it is going to show that on the video first. She is reading it. She has very difficult speech, so it is not too clear. But that was a big moment. She is reading it and she knows what it means. Oh, never want to thank me. I want to thank her... So she is reading it. It means sit on a chair. And she knew what it meant. And I was absolutely shocked.

## Loneliness, Identity, and God's Faithfulness

So another thing that I learned — and this is almost the beginning, I have not been very clear about that, I am sorry about that. But this picture was taken in what would turn out to be one of the hardest months in my life. And this was also the month that I met my future husband. So God works in times and ways that we do not expect. I think one thing that I have talked about a lot is how God works beyond what we can ask or think or imagine. And I think I learned that that can mean it is more complex than just good things. God can put us through things that we could not imagine, but he is there in the midst of them. He is working in them. And he can do beyond what we can think or imagine.

So obviously this was a huge blessing. Obviously that acts as the number one blessing. But second, I think it is important to note that God really taught me also how to be alone. This has been a really lonely part of my life. I live alone. I do everything alone. I mean, I am always around people because I make myself always be around people. But this chapter was one. And the reality of this is that once you start living in a context like this, you start really feeling like you do not belong anywhere. You never get to really belong there. And you always have these moments where the ground kind of falls out underneath you. You are like, oh, I do not belong here. And you come back and you do not feel like you still belong in your original context through your front. And that is definitely a steep season.

You have all these experiences and you are like, I cannot make people understand what I went through. And it is not because I know everybody around me wants to understand exactly what I went through. It is just that the experiences are so unique, so crazy, so different, such a different context. So learning that we have a God who walks through that with us, that does not leave us alone, when he does make us walk through that valley he is walking there with us — that was huge.

And it is like when he took Elijah on the mountaintop and Elijah is like, I am the only one. And God was there with him and he showed him that he was not the only one, that there were others like him. I think there was a really distinct moment when he showed me that. When I was in 2020 and they just shut down the borders — no one could go in or out — I may not have had plans to leave but when you know you cannot leave, and when you know no one can come in, you start to feel like everything is shrinking around you. At that time we were high-volume ministry and we had no one to help us do it. And I just felt like I was the only one left there.

I was on this Zoom prayer meeting that I only ever went to that one time. And there was this other girl there on the meeting. I had never met her, never spoken to her, never met her before or since. And she spoke — I did not speak because I was just part of the group — and she said everything that I was feeling. She was in a different country but she was in the exact same situation. And I just started crying. God showed me that I am not that alone. And we were able to pray for each other and encourage each other in a way that perhaps no one else could do for us at that time. So God is gracious in that way.

And then I just think that he has also been showing me that faithfulness can look like small, tiny steps. And sometimes it can be so overwhelming. You feel like you have to do all these big things, that you have to change the world, that you have to leave something behind. And that does not always work out. But he can still call you to be faithful to wake up every day, to love the people around you, to serve them.

Even with this project for the kids, it started with this one kid. And he hated it. He hated it. And we would go and he would scream the entire time. And I would sit next to him and I would just talk to him. I know you guys all know that I am not a patient person. But God gave me grace. And I would just sit there and he would scream, I would just talk and talk. And God showed me his kindness, I think through him specifically. Because by the end, he would go everywhere with me. He would follow me around and sit in homes with me and cuddle up next to me and just be so sweet and talk to the families who would not have accepted that four years ago. And he was a hard battle fought. But sometimes the hardest battles fought are the ones that you end up loving the most.

So God showed me that it is not always the big things. We are not always going to achieve the big things. But he is there to achieve the big things. And he is there to change lives, to make the crazy things happen. But he has called us to those small and faithful moments. Sometimes it is so hard. Sometimes it is putting one foot in front of the other, getting out of bed, moving forward. And it feels like the hardest thing we could possibly do. But he honors that. He is honored by that. And I am really thankful for that.

And I think my last thing that I want to stress is this. Before I left, my dad gave me a verse around Jeremiah 29:11, and he talked about how that verse — you know, the plans that I have for you, plans to prosper and not for evil — is so taken out of context. But yeah, it was written to people in exile. And I think in a lot of ways these last four years I felt like a sort of exile. But that is not a bad thing. We are all called to be exiles. You see in 1 Peter that he calls them exiles over and over and over again. Yes, they are exiles. But we are God's people now and we are living in this world as exiles. So to feel it in this exponential sense of being in this place — and in that chapter, Jeremiah 29, it calls to pray for the people that you are amongst. Because in your welfare there it will be their welfare. And I think I have really clung to this in this season because it has always felt like the plans were to prosper. But that is the truth. That is the reality, that God is working, God is going to prosper his people in that place, for, despite of, or because of the people there. And his name is going to be glorified. And you guys have been a part of that. You guys have shown me that. You guys have walked with me every step of the way. You guys have prayed for me. You guys have just been so instrumental.

And like I said, I will never be able to show how grateful I am to you guys. I wish I could peel back and just show you everything that happened, everything that you are part of, everything that you made possible. The normal mundane moments and the crazy big ones — because there is just so much beauty in all of that. I was able to just witness God and his beauty and his grace in ways that I just wish I had the ability to show you now. But I am thankful for you guys. And I am thankful for you guys in this new transition. Because leaving was not easy. And this new step of living life is a transition. And knowing that you guys are praying for me and are with me in this — it is just such a blessing that I am so thankful for.

So for the last time for now, I really appreciate you guys listening to me talk about all of this. Not doing any of it justice. But to be able to share with you guys all the stuff that happened.

[Questions and brief final prayer.]

Father, thank You for the fact that You are present everywhere. Thank You that You are God of big things and little things. Thank You for the ability to continue to spend these years in ministry. Father, we pray for the things that were left behind, the people left behind, the seeds left behind. We pray for this time of transition. We pray for a new chapter. And we know that You are the one who leads that, who directs that. You are righteous. You are good. In this time of transition, we pray for blessing. We pray in the name of Your Son, our Lord Jesus Christ. Amen.
